title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 10 (Fragment 2)
text:
While he thought, Rinaldo said angrily, “What possessed that stupid brat Tonino to read that blessed book? I shall give him something to think about when we get him back!”

Paolo shivered in the cold. “Tonino always reads books.” Then he shifted a bit—the elegant attitude was not at all comfortable—and asked timidly, “How shall we get him back?” This was not at all what he had planned to say. He was annoyed with himself.

“What’s the use?” Rinaldo said. “We know where he is—in the Casa Petrocchi. And if he’s uncomfortable there, it’s his own fault!”

“But he’s not!” Paolo protested. As far as he could see in the light from the yard lamp, Rinaldo turned and looked at him jeeringly. The discussion seemed to be getting further from the way he had planned it every second. “An enemy enchanter’s got him,” he said. “The one Chrestomanci talked about.”

Rinaldo laughed. “Load of old crab-apples, Paolo. Our friend had been talking to the Petrocchis. He invented his convenient enchanter because he wanted us all working for Caprona. Most of us saw through it at once.”

“Then who made that mist on the Corso?” Paolo said. “It wasn’t us, and it wasn’t them.”

But Rinaldo only said, “Who said it wasn’t them?”

As Paolo could not say it was Renata Petrocchi, he could not answer. Instead, he said rather desperately, “Come with me to the Casa Petrocchi. If you used a finding-spell, you could prove Tonino isn’t there.”

“What?” Rinaldo seemed astounded. “What kind of fool do you take me for, Paolo? I’m not going to take on a whole family of spell-makers single-handed. And if I go there and use a spell, and they do something to Tonino, everyone’s going to blame me, aren’t they? For something we know anyway. It’s not worth it, Paolo. But I tell you what—”

He was interrupted by Aunt Gina trumpeting below in the yard. “Notti’s is the only chemist open by now. Tell him it’s for Niccolo Montana!”

With some relief, Paolo dropped the elegant attitude entirely and leaned over the rail to watch Lucia and Corinna hurry through the yard with the doctor’s prescription. The sight gave his stomach a wrench of worry. “Do you think Old Niccolo’s going to die, Rinaldo?”

Rinaldo shrugged. “Could be. He’s pretty old. It’s about time the old idiot gave up anyway. I shall be one step closer to being head of the Casa Montana then.”

A peculiar thing happened inside Paolo’s head then. He had never given much thought to who might follow Antonio—for it was clear his father would follow Old Niccolo—as head of the Casa Montana. But he had never, for some reason, thought it might be Rinaldo. Now he tried to imagine Rinaldo doing the things Old Niccolo did. And as soon as he did, he saw Rinaldo was quite unsuitable. Rinaldo was vain, and selfish—and cowardly, provided he could be a coward and still keep up a good appearance. It was as if Rinaldo had said a powerful spell to clear Paolo’s eyes.

It never occurred to Rinaldo, expert spell-maker though he was, that a few ordinary words could make such a difference. He bent towards Paolo and dropped his voice to a melodious murmur. “I was going to tell you, Paolo. I’m going around enlisting all the young ones. We’re going to swear to work a secret revenge on the Petrocchis. We’ll do something worse than make them eat their words. Are you with me? Will you swear to join the plan?”

vocabulary.definition: slightly angry or irritated.
vocabulary.example: He was annoyed with himself.

vocabulary.definition: the way a person or thing looks or seems to others.
vocabulary.example: Keep up a good appearance.

vocabulary.definition: very surprised or amazed.
vocabulary.example: Rinaldo seemed astounded.

vocabulary.definition: used to express annoyance or emphasis, often ironically.
vocabulary.example: That blessed book caused a lot of trouble.

vocabulary.definition: a child, typically one who is annoying or badly behaved.
vocabulary.example: What possessed that stupid brat Tonino to read that blessed book?

vocabulary.definition: suitable for one's purposes and needs; easy to use or access.
vocabulary.example: He invented his convenient enchanter.

vocabulary.definition: lacking courage; excessively afraid of danger or pain.
vocabulary.example: Rinaldo was cowardly, provided he could keep up a good appearance.

vocabulary.definition: pleasingly graceful and stylish in appearance or manner.
vocabulary.example: The elegant attitude was not at all comfortable.

vocabulary.definition: a person who uses magic or enchantments.
vocabulary.example: An enemy enchanter’s got him.

vocabulary.definition: to sign up or recruit, especially for military or organized activities.
vocabulary.example: I’m going around enlisting all the young ones.

vocabulary.definition: to create or produce something new or false.
vocabulary.example: He invented his convenient enchanter because he wanted us all working for Caprona.

vocabulary.definition: in a mocking or sarcastic way intended to hurt or insult.
vocabulary.example: Rinaldo looked at him jeeringly.

vocabulary.definition: having a pleasant tune or sound.
vocabulary.example: Dropped his voice to a melodious murmur.

vocabulary.definition: a cloud of tiny water droplets suspended in the atmosphere near Earth’s surface reducing visibility.
vocabulary.example: Who made that mist on the Corso?

vocabulary.definition: a soft, indistinct sound made by a person or group speaking quietly or at a distance.
vocabulary.example: A melodious murmur.

vocabulary.definition: affected with a strong or overwhelming emotion or idea, often irrational or obsessive.
vocabulary.example: What possessed that stupid brat Tonino to read that blessed book?

vocabulary.definition: a written order from a doctor for medicine or treatment.
vocabulary.example: The doctor’s prescription.

vocabulary.definition: to understand the true nature of someone or something, especially when it is hidden or deceptive.
vocabulary.example: Most of us saw through it at once.

vocabulary.definition: a private act intended to retaliate or get even without others knowing.
vocabulary.example: Swear to work a secret revenge on the Petrocchis.

vocabulary.definition: to tremble slightly because of cold, fear, or excitement.
vocabulary.example: Paolo shivered in the cold.

vocabulary.definition: to raise and lower the shoulders to show indifference or lack of knowledge.
vocabulary.example: Rinaldo shrugged.

vocabulary.definition: done without help from anyone else.
vocabulary.example: Not going to take on a whole family of spell-makers single-handed.

vocabulary.definition: a person who creates or casts magic spells.
vocabulary.example: A whole family of spell-makers.

vocabulary.definition: to make a solemn promise or assertion.
vocabulary.example: Will you swear to join the plan?

vocabulary.definition: in a way that shows a lack of confidence or courage; shyly.
vocabulary.example: He asked timidly, how shall we get him back?

vocabulary.definition: showing too much pride in one's appearance or achievements.
vocabulary.example: Rinaldo was vain, and selfish—and cowardly.

vocabulary.definition: a sudden sharp pain or emotional distress.
vocabulary.example: The sight gave his stomach a wrench of worry.
